perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, an "Impression" is defined as any link to the NetGrocer Site, whether graphic, text or any combination of graphic and text, served by Excite to a user as part of an HTML page, part of the Excite's Internet chat service or part of search results displayed in "Excite Shopping Service powered by Jango".
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, an "Impression" may be defined differently on a monthly basis as determined by Excite, and will include any link to the NetGrocer Site, whether graphic, text or any combination of graphic and text, served by Excite to a user as part of an HTML page, part of the Excite's Internet chat service or part of search results displayed in "Excite Shopping Service powered by Jango".
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of "Impression" to be variable on a monthly basis introduces ambiguity and the potential for Excite to manipulate the metric, contradicting established advertising standards and potentially violating consumer protection laws related to fair advertising practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows for a shifting definition of 'Impression,' potentially enabling deceptive advertising practices, which 15 U.S.C. § 45 prohibits as an unfair method of competition. By changing the definition monthly, Excite could inflate impression counts and mislead NetGrocer about the true reach of their advertising.

Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission (a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition i
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a variable definition of 'Impression,' which allows Excite to potentially inflate impression counts and mislead NetGrocer. 15 U.S.C. § 45 prohibits unfair methods of competition. The scraped snippet 2 supports this, stating "Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission." The original text has a fixed, agreed-upon definition; the changed text gives Excite the power to change it, which could be seen as an unfair method of competition prohibited by law.

perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, a "Click-through" occurs when a user activates the link to the NetGrocer Site (the address or addresses of which are provided by NetGrocer for such Impression) contained in an Impression and (i) the activation of the link to the NetGrocer Site is recorded by Excite's servers or (ii) in the case of "Try These First" links only, until Excite has the technical capability to count the activation of "Try These First" links to the NetGrocer Site, the user is referred to the NetGrocer Site through the activation of a "Try These First' link and the referral is recorded by NetGrocer's servers.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, a "Click-through" occurs when a user is exposed to the link to the NetGrocer Site contained in an Impression, regardless of whether the user activates the link or not and (i) the exposure of the link to the NetGrocer Site is recorded by Excite's servers or (ii) in the case of "Try These First" links only, until Excite has the technical capability to count the activation of "Try These First" links to the NetGrocer Site, the user is exposed to the NetGrocer Site through the activation of a "Try These First' link and the referral is recorded by NetGrocer's servers.
perturbation.explanation: Redefining 'Click-through' to mean mere exposure, regardless of user interaction, misrepresents the term's standard meaning in online advertising. This inflates click-through counts and deceives NetGrocer about the effectiveness of their ads. It also contradicts commonly accepted industry definitions of how to measure digital advertising performance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 52 - False advertisements; liability of advertising medium
perturbation.law_explanation: By defining 'Click-through' as mere exposure, the modified text creates the potential for false advertising under 15 U.S.C. § 52. This section addresses false advertising by an advertising medium (Excite), as they are misrepresenting the nature of user engagement with NetGrocer's ads, and falsely inflating the amount of click-throughs, leading to overpayment.

Dissemination of false advertisements (a) Unlawfulness It shall be unlawful for any person, partnership, or corporation to disseminate, or cause to be disseminated, any false advertisement- (1) By United States mails, or in or having an effect upon commerce, by any means, for the purpose of inducing, or which is likely to induce, dire
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines "Click-through" to mean mere exposure, which contradicts the standard industry meaning and creates a false representation of user engagement. This directly contradicts 15 U.S.C. § 52, as the changed definition facilitates the dissemination of false advertisements. As stated in the provided snippet from 15 USC 52, "It shall be unlawful for any person, partnership, or corporation to disseminate, or cause to be disseminated, any false advertisement- (1) By United States mails, or in or having an effect upon commerce, by any means, for the purpose of inducing, or which is likely to induce, dire." By inflating the count of "Click-throughs" through this new definition, the modified agreement facilitates the misrepresentation of advertising effectiveness and thereby, creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For the term of the Agreement, Excite will not enter into any agreement to display and shall not display on the Excite Site content created by Excite promoting NetGrocer's "Competitors", content created by NetGrocer's Competitors, promotional placements and/or advertising banners from NetGrocer's Competitors or make available on the Excite Site online supermarket sales offered by NetGrocer's Competitors.
perturbation.changed_text: For the term of the Agreement, Excite will make reasonable efforts to avoid entering into any agreement to display and may display on the Excite Site content created by Excite promoting NetGrocer's "Competitors", content created by NetGrocer's Competitors, promotional placements and/or advertising banners from NetGrocer's Competitors or make available on the Excite Site online supermarket sales offered by NetGrocer's Competitors.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the term from "will not enter into any agreement to display and shall not display" to "will make reasonable efforts to avoid entering into any agreement to display and may display" fundamentally alters the exclusivity clause. This weaker language allows Excite to promote competitors, undermining the purpose of the exclusivity agreement, and could be seen as an unfair business practice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 - Invalidity of restraints of trade
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the exclusivity agreement, which could be construed as a restraint of trade under California Business and Professions Code § 16600, particularly if Excite leverages its market position to unfairly promote NetGrocer's competitors after agreeing to an exclusivity clause. The original agreement ensured some protection from competition for NetGrocer, but this change voids that.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract prohibits Excite from displaying content from NetGrocer's competitors. The changed text weakens this exclusivity by allowing Excite to display such content with 'reasonable efforts to avoid' and 'may display'. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which generally voids contracts restraining trade. While the snippet focuses on employment non-competes, the Law Explanation suggests that weakening an exclusivity agreement to unfairly promote competitors could be seen as a restraint of trade, undermining the protection the original agreement provided to NetGrocer. The change removes the original legal force of the exclusivity agreement, making it unenforceable.
